title: Uncial alphabet — the complete letterforms
subtitle: The insular half-uncial hand, a-z, with stroke order and nib angle for each letter
excerpt: Uncial is one of the oldest continuous western calligraphic hands: a rounded, upright script with no ascenders or descenders, used in Irish and Anglo-Saxon manuscripts from the fourth century onward. This tutorial teaches all 26 letters with the broad-edge pen at 20 degrees.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on round hands and historical scripts; the uncial analysis follows Johnston's broad-edge method applied to insular half-uncial models. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rounded, upright calligraphic hand without ascenders or descenders, used in Latin and Insular manuscripts from the fourth to ninth centuries. The broad-edge pen is held at a low nib angle.
The angle of the broad-edge nib relative to the writing line. For uncial, the nib sits at roughly 20 degrees from the baseline, almost flat.
The height of the main body of a lowercase letter, measured in nib widths. Uncial uses an x-height of 4 nib widths.

content.text: The
uncial

hand is named for the inch-high letters in early Latin manuscripts; the insular half-uncial variant, used in the Book of Kells and the Lindisfarne Gospels, is the form most associated with Irish and Anglo-Saxon manuscript culture. The letters are compact and rounded, with a
nib angle
of 20 degrees and an
x-height
of 4 nib widths. There are no true ascenders or descenders: the entire alphabet sits within four nib-width lines.
What you need

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, No. 3 (the medium size)
Iron-gall or sumi ink (thin but opaque consistency)
items.qty: Small amount in a shallow dish

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm (ruled in pencil before practising)
Metal ruler, for ruling guide lines
Tracing paper (optional, for overlaying over a printed exemplar)

content.text: Setting up the guide lines

Rule four parallel lines per writing line: baseline, x-height (4 nib widths above the baseline), a line 1 nib width above x-height (the highest point of any uncial letter), and a descender line 2 nib widths below the baseline. The 4 nib-width x-height is measured by holding the nib sideways and stepping four nib-width squares up from the baseline.
Nib angle and pen hold

Hold the straight pen holder between index finger and middle finger, with the thumb supporting from the side. Rest the hand on the little finger or a piece of tracing-paper guard. The nib should sit at 20 degrees from the baseline: almost flat, but angled slightly so the broad edge is not perfectly horizontal. At this low angle, uncial letters produce thick horizontals and thin verticals, the opposite of Foundational's proportions.
Letter groups and stroke order
Uncial letters are grouped by the shapes they share.
Round letters: o, c, e, d, g, q

Start with o: two strokes. Stroke 1 is a curved stroke from the 11 o'clock position, sweeping left and down to the baseline in a wide arc. Stroke 2 closes the right side, from the 1 o'clock position sweeping right and down, meeting stroke 1 at the baseline and at the top. The letter should be a nearly complete circle. c and e follow the same arc for stroke 1; e adds a horizontal stroke in the middle of the body.
Arched letters: n, m, h, p, b

For n: stroke 1 is a vertical downstroke from the x-height to the baseline. Stroke 2 is an arched stroke that branches from the top of stroke 1 at about two-thirds of the x-height and curves outward and down to the baseline, ending with a small foot. The arch is rounder than Foundational's arch because the nib angle is lower.
Diagonal letters: v, w, x, y, z

For v: stroke 1 is a thin diagonal from the x-height to the baseline (the pen moving down-and-right, the narrow edge leading). Stroke 2 is a broader diagonal from the x-height down to meet stroke 1 at the baseline (the pen moving down-and-left, the broad edge leading). The two strokes share a baseline point.
Unique forms: a, r, s, t, f, i, j, k, l, u

Uncial a is the rounded single-storey form: the bowl is nearly a full circle (three strokes: the arc, the inner curve, and the downstroke). Uncial r has a short curved arm that does not reach as far as Foundational r. Uncial t has a very low crossbar sitting just above mid-x-height; the vertical extends only one nib width above it. Insular f descends below the baseline by 2 nib widths; its crossbar sits at the baseline, not across the body.
Common mistakes and how to avoid them

attrs.heading: Common uncial errors
attrs.intro: Errors that appear in the first few practice sheets.

items.symptom: Letters look narrow and upright rather than wide and round
items.cause: Nib angle is too steep (above 30 degrees), tilting the broad edge toward the vertical and producing narrower strokes.
items.fix: Lower the nib angle toward 20 degrees. The nib should feel almost horizontal. Run a test stroke on scrap paper and check that horizontals are the thickest parts.

items.symptom: Ink spreads into the paper at the entry point of each stroke
items.cause: Too much ink loaded on the nib, or paper too absorbent.
items.fix: Load ink more sparingly. Slightly resize the practice paper with a dilute coat of gum sandarac or switch to a harder-surfaced sheet.

items.symptom: Letters in the same word are inconsistent in height
items.cause: Writing on unruled paper, or not following the guide lines while practising.
items.fix: Rule every practice sheet and follow the lines deliberately. At this stage, accuracy matters more than flow.
